🇸🇰 SK

ECB SAFE Survey — Wave 38 · Slovakia

Slovakia · Euro Area · Germany  |  Generated 06 Jul 2026

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Executive Summary

  • Margin squeeze intensifies: Slovak turnover contracted for a net 26% of firms while labour costs rose to a net 84% and profits fell to a net 40% (up 17.4 pp) — with expected input prices (8.3%) far outpacing selling prices (4.3%), compressing margins from both directions.
  • Short-term vs term financing split: The Slovak credit line financing gap widened to +14.8pp (from +4.8pp) on surging short-term need, while bank loan conditions stayed favourable at a negative -4.0pp gap — a sharp divergence between liquidity instruments and term lending.
  • Turnover outlook diverges: A net 7% of Slovak firms expected turnover to increase, down sharply by 14.15 pp, while the euro area rose to a net 26% — a divergence of nearly 19 pp pointing to weaker domestic demand conditions.
  • Interest rates turn tighter: A net 5% of Slovak firms reported tighter interest rate conditions — a 13.8 pp swing from the prior quarter's net 8% easing — though tightening remained well below the euro area's net 27%.

Contents

    • Credit line stress widened notably as demand surged, diverging from easy bank loan conditions

    • Regulation and production costs jointly top Slovak firms' concerns in wave 38

Financing Conditions

Slovak interest rate conditions tightened notably, reversing prior quarter's easing

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=62) reported tighter interest rate conditions — a swing of 13.8 pp from the prior quarter's net 8% easing — though SK tightening remained well below the EA's net 27% and DE's net 38%, suggesting Slovak firms face relatively less pressure compared to peers.
  • Non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions) remained persistently elevated, with a net 41% of Slovak firms (n=60) reporting increases, broadly stable (easing marginally by 1.8 pp vs wave 37) and above the EA's net 37%, putting further strain on firm profitability.
  • Collateral requirements and other loan terms tightened only marginally for Slovak firms (net 1% and net 8%, respectively), remaining notably below DE levels of net 32% and net 33%, indicating Slovak firms face comparatively less restrictive lending conditions.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Credit line stress widened notably as demand surged, diverging from easy bank loan conditions

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • The Slovak credit line financing gap widened notably to +14.8pp (n=278) from +4.8pp in the prior quarter, driven almost entirely by rising need (from +3.1pp to +12.3pp), while availability moved only marginally (from -1.7pp to -2.5pp) — suggesting Slovak firms face heightened short-term liquidity strain compared to the euro area's +12.6pp.
  • By contrast, Slovak bank loan conditions remained favourable: the gap held negative at -4.0pp (n=254), with availability steady at +1.4pp — 5.7pp less stressed than the euro area's +1.7pp gap — underscoring a sharp divergence between short-term liquidity instruments and term lending.
  • Trade credit stress eased moderately, with the Slovak gap narrowing from +9.8pp to +6.3pp (n=150), as need fell from +10.1pp to +7.1pp and availability edged up from +0.3pp to +0.8pp — indicating Slovak firms are now less exposed to trade credit constraints than the euro area's +8.2pp.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak firms' bank loan availability expectations deteriorated moderately in wave 38

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • Slovak firms' expectations for trade credit availability remained notably more pessimistic than the EA, with a net 10% expecting deterioration (n=79) versus a net 1% in the EA — a gap of 9.4 pp — though conditions improved marginally by 1.5 pp from the previous quarter's net 12%, suggesting tighter liquidity constraints ahead.
  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=130) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate over the coming quarters, worsening by 3.2 pp from a net 2% in the prior wave, broadly in line with the EA's parallel deterioration of 3.4 pp to a net 4%, putting pressure on investment plans.
  • Expectations for credit line and bank overdraft availability also edged lower for Slovak firms, with a net 7% expecting deterioration (n=138) — a 1.3 pp decline from the previous quarter — compared with a net 4% in the EA, leaving a moderate 3.3 pp gap, signalling tighter short-term financing conditions.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms relied heavily on working capital financing, diverging from euro area peers

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • Inventory and working capital remained the dominant financing purpose for Slovak firms, cited by 55.7% (n=229) — 16.2 pp above the euro area's 39.5% — with the share virtually unchanged from wave 37 (Δ=−0.1 pp), indicating a persistent short-term liquidity focus.
  • Financing for hiring and training rose from 26.9% to 31.1% (Δ=+4.2 pp, n=227) among Slovak firms, while the euro area share fell from 27.4% to 24.3%; financing for new product development also outpaced the EA at 32.8% vs 20.2%, widening the gap by 2.9 pp.
  • Fixed investment dropped from 40.3% to 34.2% (Δ=−6.1 pp, n=229) among Slovak firms, falling below the euro area's 36.8% — reversing the position from wave 37, when SK marginally exceeded EA on this purpose, signalling a shift away from long-term growth investments.

Bank willingness positive for Slovak firms but edging lower

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net 7.8% of Slovak firms (n=306) reported that banks' willingness to provide credit improved, easing marginally by 2.6 pp from the prior quarter's net 10.4% — yet remaining above the euro area's net 5.5% and well above Germany's net -4.4%, suggesting continued but weakening support for external financing.
  • Access to public financial support deteriorated notably for Slovak firms (n=298), with a net 33.8% reporting worsening conditions — an 8.8 pp decline from the previous quarter's net 25.0%, and materially worse than the euro area's net 15.5%, putting pressure on firms reliant on state-backed funding.
  • The general economic outlook continued to deteriorate for Slovak firms (n=442), with a net 44.4% reporting a worsening outlook — 6.1 pp more negative than the prior quarter and 18.3 pp below the euro area's already negative net 26.1%, indicating heightened macroeconomic headwinds for financing decisions.

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak firm profits deteriorated notably as turnover declined and costs surged

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net 39.80% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported falling profits in wave 38, worsening by 17.37 pp from a net 22% in the prior quarter — more than double the euro area's net 17% profit decline, signalling weaker financial performance for Slovak businesses.
  • Slovak turnover contracted for a net 26% of firms (n=229), deepening 11.60 pp from a net 15% decline in wave 37, while the euro area remained broadly flat at a net 1% increase, reducing revenue streams for Slovak firms.
  • Labour cost pressures intensified, with a net 84% of Slovak firms (n=229) reporting higher labour costs — up 21.78 pp from the prior quarter and exceeding the euro area's net 63% — compressing margins alongside rising other costs at a net 72%, squeezing profitability further.

Slovak turnover outlook fell sharply while EA firms grew more optimistic

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 7% of Slovak firms (n=228) expected turnover to increase over coming quarters, falling sharply by 14.15 pp from the net 21% in the previous wave, while the euro area's net balance rose 2.72 pp to net 26% — a divergence of nearly 19 pp, suggesting weaker domestic demand conditions for Slovak firms.
  • Slovak fixed investment expectations improved moderately, rising from a net 11% to a net 15% (Δ +3.88 pp), outpacing the euro area which eased from a net 13% to a net 9%, reversing relative positions between the two, indicating potential confidence in local business conditions despite regional headwinds.
  • German firms reported a net negative fixed investment balance of 2% (Δ −4.50 pp), contrasting with Slovakia's positive net 15%, underscoring divergent capital expenditure outlooks across the region — highlighting Slovakia's relatively stronger investment sentiment.

Slovak firms face margin compression as input costs dwarf selling price expectations

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • Slovak firms (n=417) anticipated non-labour input price rises of 8.31% over the next 12 months — nearly double the EA's 5.84% — while expected selling prices rose only 4.27% (n=418), leaving a margin gap of over 4 pp — squeezing profitability as cost increases outpace pricing power.
  • Wage growth expectations among Slovak firms eased moderately to 4.21% (n=441, down 1.28 pp from the prior quarter's 5.49%), yet remained 1.41 pp above the EA average of 2.80%, maintaining pressure on labour cost margins — limiting room for investment or hiring.
  • Slovak firms' five-year inflation expectations rose to 8.57% (n=163, up 1.51 pp vs wave 37), exceeding the EA's 6.28% by the widest margin across all forecast horizons surveyed — signaling persistent cost concerns that may deter long-term planning.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms retain the highest upside inflation risk balance across three economies

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 59% of Slovak firms (n=229) viewed five-year inflation risks as skewed to the upside, exceeding the euro area's net 55% (n=5,278) and Germany's net 51%, retaining the widest upside bias across the three economies in wave 38 — underscoring heightened pricing pressures for Slovak businesses.
  • The euro area's upside inflation risk balance rose notably by 9.90 pp from the prior wave to net 55%, narrowing the SK–EA gap from 7.7 pp in wave 37 to 3.6 pp — as EA firms' inflation concerns catch up with Slovak firms', reducing the relative advantage Slovak firms previously held.
  • Slovak firms' own upside balance rose moderately by 5.80 pp from net 53% in wave 37 to net 59% in wave 38, a smaller acceleration than the euro area's, yet confirming persistent local price-risk concerns on the upside — suggesting sustained uncertainty over input and output pricing strategies.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Regulation and production costs jointly top Slovak firms' concerns in wave 38

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Costs of production or labour and regulation were virtually tied as the most pressing problems among the seven tested for Slovak firms, scoring 6.19/10 (n=228) and 6.18/10 (n=225) respectively — both moderate on the 1–10 scale — while skilled staff availability, at 6.07/10, ranked third, suggesting firms face dual cost pressures that may constrain investment or hiring.
  • Regulation rose marginally by 0.12 points from wave 37 for Slovak firms (n=225), diverging from the EA trend where regulation eased from 6.04 to 5.89/10; Slovak firms thus rated regulation more pressingly than the EA average in wave 38, indicating a growing compliance burden relative to peers.
  • Availability of skilled staff or experienced managers recorded the largest single-problem decline for Slovakia, easing by 0.50 points from 6.57 to 6.07/10 (n=228), though it remained above the EA's own easing from 6.64 to 6.51/10, reflecting a persistent labour market challenge despite some improvement.

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
